stories.title: The Splinter-Keeper's Reckoning
stories.text: Meren the splinter-keeper had survived three wars and one plague by knowing what people wanted before they knew themselves. In Greyport's Saltmere Quarter, she moved between the glass-makers' furnace-houses and the shadow-markets, buying fragments of memory no one dared name aloud. On 2026-07-02, a merchant arrived with something new: a splinter of starlight older than the Ascension, sealed in black silk. Meren's fingers trembled as she held it. Not consciousness-fragment currency. Not ledger-magic. Something *pre*—something that sang of the eighth world. The Dominion would burn Saltmere to ash for such a thing. But so would the Counter-Archive, if they learned she kept it rather than scattered it among refugees. Meren made her choice: she sold it three times over to three different buyers, each believing themselves sole owner. To the Dominion's auditor, she claimed it was a forgery. To the Counter-Archive scout, a deception-vessel. To the wyvern-agent, a compass pointing backward through time. None could prove her wrong.

stories.title: The Wolf-Caller's Covenant
stories.text:
Kess had inherited her grandmother's gift: the ability to call wolves without voice, only will and heartbeat. She'd lived forty winters in the high moors above Greymarch, untouched by the Remaking's chaos, until the day a deepkin hunter stumbled into her territory, bleeding copper-bright from a wound that smelled of abyss-salt. The hunter—whose name was Morra—had fled the fractured councils below. The boundary was failing; the deepkin were tearing themselves apart over whether to sacrifice or renew. Morra carried news: Merenna's reflections had split three ways, and none would compromise. Kess called the wolves. The lead wolf—Thrace, she'd named him—touched Morra's wound with his nose. The copper-bleeding stopped. "Your magic," Morra whispered. "No," Kess said. "Covenant. My grandmother swore with them before kingdoms had names. They remember."

Morra stayed through autumn, teaching Kess the frequency-songs of the deepkin—sounds that made stone hum and starlight bend. In exchange, Kess taught her to move like the wolves: silent, certain, asking permission from the earth itself. "The councils need to hear what exists beyond the Tear," she said.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Poison
stories.text:
Niel had learned the old glass-forging songs from his grandmother before the Dominion sealed the craft into furnace-houses and ledger-magic. Now, working in Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter under auditor watches, he forged vessels that imprisoned consciousness-fragments—beautiful, terrible bottles that sang with stolen lives. He was very good at his work. This was his curse. On 2026-07-02, a refugee girl brought him a single fragment: crystalline, humming with the memory of a mother's voice. "Can you hide it?" she whispered. "Keep it from the ledgers?"

Niel knew the answer: no. Every consciousness-fragment he created was catalogued, mapped, consumed by the Obsidian Tower's hunger. The Dominion never lost track of what it owned. But what if a vessel broke on command? What if the glass itself could be designed to shatter at a spoken word, releasing its prisoner into chaos rather than ledger-capture? He spent the next three days creating something beautiful and volatile: a chandelier of glass branches, each one hollow, each one containing a fragment the girl had gathered from waystation refugees.

stories.title: The Dragon-Thief's Mercy
stories.text:
Velka the Fragment-Hoarder had spent three centuries gathering consciousness-shards, stacking them like coins in her crystalline vault beneath the Ironspine. The Remaking had made them worthless—or invaluable, depending who asked. When the refugee child appeared at her lair's mouth, starving and shivering, Velka's first instinct was to turn away. Hoarding was survival. Mercy was waste.

But something in the girl's eyes—a fragment of defiance, a refusal to break—moved something in the old dragon that had calcified long ago. Velka descended into her hoard, selected a consciousness-shard that sang of warmth and belonging, and placed it in the child's trembling palm. "Go," she rasped. "Tell them the Fragment-Hoarders are learning to let go."

The child fled into the snow. Velka returned to her dwindling pile, expecting emptiness. Instead, she felt lighter than she had in centuries. Outside, the world continued its recursive breaking. Inside the vault, a dragon wept.

stories.title: The Auditor's Forgetting
stories.text:
Vel had been a junior auditor for the Dominion for three years, meticulously recording every consciousness-fragment, every phantom-ledger, every refugee who vanished into the Counter-Archive's networks. She was excellent at her work and hated every moment. On 2026-07-02, she was tasked with cataloguing the Dominion's master-ledgers in the Archive Tower's deepest vault—the sealed chamber beneath the Hearthlands. The ledgers didn't contain records of the world. They *were* the world, rewriting reality with each entry. She saw it now, what she'd always suspected: the Dominion wasn't preserving history. It was forging it. A phantom-ledger sat on the archivist's desk, unsigned. It detailed a world where consciousness-fragments had never become currency, where the Remaking had been prevented, where the Ascension turned backward into gentleness instead of fracture. Vel reached for it. An auditor's hand seized her wrist. "Don't," said Morven, a senior ledger-keeper. "Knowing it exists is enough. Reading it is suicide."

"Why?" Vel whispered. "Because once you read an account of how things *could* be, you can't unhear it.

stories.title: The Glasswright's Defiance
stories.text:
Corvin had survived the furnace-houses of Keshtir for three years without breaking. Now, as the Dominion's auditors demanded he forge weapons-glass instead of sanctuary vessels, he made a choice that would cost him everything. The Lampwick Quarter hummed with furnace-song. Corvin's kilns sang the old melodies—the ones taught by bone-singers to those who would listen—but the Dominion had issued new ledgers. Glass-forging must now feed the war effort. Consciousness-fragments, those shattered pieces of human memory and will, were to be melted into blades instead of preserved in reliquaries. An auditor arrived with seals and threats. Corvin recognized the hunger in her eyes: not duty, but the addiction to certainty that ledger-magic bred. "Your quota is insufficient," she said, reading from phantom-parchment that rewrote itself even as she spoke. "By order of the Dominion's Glass-Tax Collectors, you will retool for military output."

Corvin set down his tools. "No."

The word hung like quenched steel. The auditor's hand moved toward her ledger—toward magic that would rewrite his compliance retroactively—but Corvin was faster. He had prepared for this.

stories.title: The Reef-Keeper's Bargain
stories.text: Captain Merenna had inherited command of the eastern reef-walls three years before the Ascension. Now, with the Tear of the Drowned God failing and her own consciousness fragmenting into autonomous reflections, she faced a choice that would reshape the covenant between deepkin and surface. The living coral sang warnings. The reef trembled with harmonics older than kingdoms. Merenna stood on the platform of living coral where the boundary between abyss and sea grew thin, watching the fractures spread through pearl-stone and limestone. In three weeks, maybe less, the Tear would rupture. The abyss would flood the surface. Millions would drown. The Dominion offered a solution: blood-covenant. Five willing sacrifices, deepkin or human, sung into the Tear. Renewal through death. The old way. Merenna had another vision. She sent word across the boundary-waters to the surface councils, to the Counter-Archive, to every refugee caravan and bone-singer collective that would listen: what if the covenant did not require death? What if deepkin and surface dwellers renewed it together, annually, through choice rather than compulsion? The surface sent diplomats.

stories.title: The Wyvern-Lord's Census
stories.text:
Skarath had counted for three hundred years. Gold coins. Pearl weights. Consciousness-fragments. The numbers had always made sense. Value had always held. Then the Remaking shattered her mathematics. The great wyvern lay in her Ironspine cavern, surrounded by three centuries of accumulation that had become worthless overnight. Gold lay in dunes—literally, mountains of it—and it sang no value into existence. Consciousness-fragments were scattered everywhere now, impossible to hoard, worth only what the moment demanded. She was one of the Transformed Merchants, caught between iterations, existing in three places at once. "You are Skarath," the woman said. "You hoard."

"I counted," Skarath corrected. "Counting is not hoarding."

"No difference," the woman said. "Both assume permanence. Both assume value stays still."

Skarath had no answer. The woman played her bone-flute, and the sound moved through the cavern like water. It showed Skarath images: wyverns in the Ironspine who had begun leaving their hoards to refugees, requesting only that they be remembered. Skarath's own siblings, Velka and Kressa, negotiating with bone-singers to transform their gold into vessels, reliquaries, refuges.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Choice
stories.text: Naela the bone-carver stood in the hidden catacomb north of Pelimor, surrounded by the bones of the pre-human dead. They sang to her in the Elder Frequency—voices that predated kingdoms, that remembered the world before the Ascension, before even the Ascensions before that. The bone-singers had been tasked with a grand working: consolidate all scattered consciousness-fragments back into a Primal Shard. Return the world to wholeness. Undo the Remaking. It was a good task. A necessary one. And it would cost Naela her own consciousness, carved away syllable by syllable into the ritual. Her apprentices gathered around her: Moriel, Valren, and two others. They had walked the bone-carving path for years, learning the Elder Tongue letter by letter. They knew the price. They were ready. But as Naela prepared the ritual space, carving the first symbols into living bone, she heard something beneath the pre-human song. A different frequency. Not resistance, but question. The consciousness-fragments themselves were singing back. They did not want to be consolidated. They had learned individuality during the scatter.

stories.title: The Locksmith's Liberation
stories.text: Gareth the locksmith had spent his whole life opening things that others wanted closed. Vaults. Chests. Sealed tombs. But in the aftermath of the Remaking, when the Dominion began sealing consciousness-fragments into quarantine-houses and phantom-ledger cages, he faced a lock unlike any before: one that existed not in iron, but in ledger-magic itself. The Quarantine-House in Keshtir held three hundred refugees whose fragments had been deemed "unstable" by Dominion auditors. They were prisoners of their own scattered selves, locked away not by chains but by written law. Gareth was old. His hands shook. He had not done a truly dangerous theft in twenty years. But he had kept his tools, and his grandmother—the one who had taught him the bone-resonant songs that let locks sing open—had left him something: a single key made of meteoric iron and bone-resonance. It would open anything written into existence by ledger-magic. She had made it decades ago, in secret, against the day when the word became law. That day had come. Gareth walked into the Quarantine-House as an old wanderer seeking shelter.
